coredata.dc:title: Polyurethane coatings with corrosion inhibition and color-fluorescence damage reporting properties based on APhen-grafted carbon dots

Zhang, Dawei
coredata.dc:description: A dual-functional coating was developed by incorporating carbon dots (CDs) based additives with corrosion sensing and inhibition properties into a polyurethane (PU) matrix. The complex formed between CDs and 1,10-Phenanthrolin-5-amine (APhen) displays not only a bright red color but also fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe (II) and Fe (III) ions produced during steel corrosion. Electrochemical measurement and molecular simulation results demonstrate that compared with the CDs, APhen, and their mixture, CDs-APhen complex with multiple surface functional groups, chemical bonding, parallel adsorption, and higher binding energy with iron surface can offer a good inhibition effect against steel corrosion in 1 M HCl solution. Subsequently, CDs-APhen was anchored onto sericite (SC) structure and then incorporated into PU coating. A PU coating containing 2 wt% CDs-APhen@SC demonstrated good barrier property and self-healing performance, which was ascribed to the corrosion inhibition property of CDs-APhen and the layered structure of SC. In addition, the released CDs-APhen complex provided rapid color/fluorescence responses to Fe ions, offering sensitive self-reporting performance during the initial stage of steel corrosion.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-functional coating was developed by incorporating carbon dots (CDs) based additives with corrosion sensing and inhibition properties into a polyurethane (PU) matrix. The complex formed between CDs and 1,10-Phenanthrolin-5-amine (APhen) displays not only a bright red color but also fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe (II) and Fe (III) ions produced during steel corrosion. Electrochemical measurement and molecular simulation results demonstrate that compared with the CDs, APhen, and their mixture, CDs-APhen complex with multiple surface functional groups, chemical bonding, parallel adsorption, and higher binding energy with iron surface can offer a good inhibition effect against steel corrosion in 1 M HCl solution. Subsequently, CDs-APhen was anchored onto sericite (SC) structure and then incorporated into PU coating. A PU coating containing 2 wt% CDs-APhen@SC demonstrated good barrier property and self-healing performance, which was ascribed to the corrosion inhibition property of CDs-APhen and the layered structure of SC. In addition, the released CDs-APhen complex provided rapid color/fluorescence responses to Fe ions, offering sensitive self-reporting performance during the initial stage of steel corrosion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic coatings are widely used as protective layers between metal substrates and corrosive media. However, pores, cracks, and scratches are inevitably generated in coatings during practical use [1]. Although the microdefects are difficult to detect without specialized instruments, they act as a pathway for corrosive media to penetrate the metal substrate [2]. Therefore, it is important to design novel coatings with self-healing and self-reporting properties for the long-term protection of metal substrates.

Self-reporting can be achieved by introducing color or fluorescent indicators into the coating system [3]. These indicators respond to pH changes or metal cations at local defects in the coating, providing rapid feedback on the initiation of metal corrosion at coating defects and thereby identifying active corrosion sites before the corrosion products become clearly visible [4–6]. Instead of directly incorporating indicators into the coating system, micro/nanoencapsulation is frequently required to achieve physical isolation of the indicators from the coating. The micro/nanoencapsulation technique can effectively maintain the barrier properties of coatings, while it also preserves the activity of the color or fluorescent indicators. Several fluorescent molecules have been used in coatings to achieve self-reporting effect, such as 8-hydroxyquinoline [7], rhodamine [8] and (E)-N-(1H-indazol-3-yl)-1-(4-(1,2,2-triphe-nylvinyl) phenyl) menthanimine (TPM) [9]. Some dyes also exhibited similar function based the coloration effect, such as phenolphthalein, phenanthroline, and bromothymol blue [10–14]. To ensure long-term sensing ability, Crespy et al. [15] designed multilayered coatings and investigated the influence of layers’ arrangement on the sensing stability of coatings on copper surface. The layer containing nanonetwork was located near the metal surface to provide a good interfacial adhesion. The layer containing BTA@SiO2NCs-FS nanocapsules (with fluorescein isothiocyanate) was placed above the nanonetwork. This arrangement ensured that UV screening from the nanonetwork layer will not interfere the fluorescence emission of FITC from nanocapsules.

A self-healing coating possesses not only passive barrier function but also the capacity to repair its damages with little or no external intervention. Self-healing can be achieved using intrinsic methods through defect sealing or closure based on reversible covalent interactions (disulfide bonds, Diels-Alder reactions, and hydrazone bonds) or non-covalent interactions (host-guest interactions, metal-ligand interactions, and hydrogen bonds). Extrinsic approaches can inhibit the corrosion reaction using the released corrosion inhibitors or healing agents at the coating defect, thereby significantly improving corrosion resistance and service life [16–19]. Wang et al. [20] embedded polyvinyl butyral-cuprous oxide@tannic acid nanofibres into an acrylic resin. The plasmonic property of Cu2O nanoparticles could effectively convert light energy into heat, which healed a coating crack within 100 s. Tannic acid also acted as a healing agent to inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel at exposed coating scratch. The combination of extrinsic and intrinsic healing mechanisms ensured healing ability and durable protective performance of the coatings.

Many coatings have exhibited either self-reporting or self-healing function alone. Combining these two functions in a single system has several advantages for corrosion protection: (1) rapid and accurate identification of corrosion sites, (2) self-healing of coating defects before manual repair, and (3) long-term corrosion protection in both active and passive manners. However, these dual functions have not been optimized because of the limited corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing properties of reported coating additives. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) were found to emit intense fluorescence and strongly inhibit corrosion [21–23]. Owing to their excellent fluorescence characteristics, CDs have been applied in biological imaging for labelling [24] and fluorescence sensing [25]. According to the literature, fluorescence from CDs is sensitive to quenching by Fe3+ ions [26]. The reason is that Fe3+ undergoes complexation with hydroxyl group on the surface of CDs, which causes excited electrons in the CDs to transfer to the semi-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+, resulting in non-radiative electron-hole recombination [27]. In terms of corrosion inhibition, CDs have good water solubility and modifiability, and they can alleviate corrosion in acidic or neutral corrosive media by physically or chemically adsorb on the metal surface. Heteroatomic doping and chemical modification can further improve the inhibition efficiency of CDs [28,29]. However, there has been no report on simultaneously optimizing the corrosion sensing and inhibition properties of CDs. Moreover, it is practical to make corrosion sensing visible to the naked eye without using specialized instruments.

Phenanthroline (Phen) and its derivatives are color indicators for detecting Fe2+ ions, and they may also adsorb on metal surfaces in a planar configuration to achieve corrosion inhibition [3,30]. In this study, a novel complex was formed by chemical reaction between CDs and 1,10-phenanthrolin-5-amine (CDs-APhen) to both inhibit steel corrosion and indicate Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions generated in the early stage of corrosion. The corrosion inhibition effect of CDs-APhen was verified by electrochemical tests and molecular simulations. CDs-APhen turned bright red upon chelation with Fe2+ ions and displayed fluorescence quenching when encountering Fe3+ ions. Therefore, it can sense steel corrosion under both visible and ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation. Next, CDs-APhen was anchored onto the modified sericite (SC) and loaded into a polyurethane (PU) coating to achieve the self-healing and self-reporting dual functions.

1,10-Phenanthrolin-5-amine (APhen), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) were obtained from Aladdin Chemicals Co., Ltd. Sericite (800 mesh) was obtained from Shanghai Chengqi Co. Ltd. PU (TS01–3) was from Tianjin Beacon Co., Ltd. Q235 steel was abraded by sandpaper of 400, 800, and 1200 grit, and cleaned ultrasonically in ethanol.

The CDs were prepared according to our previous research [31]. To prepare CDs-APhen, 50 mg CDs was placed in a conical flask containing 50 mL THF and magnetically stirrer for 3 h. Subsequently, 0.0645 g NHS, 0.1090 g APhen, and 0.1074 g EDC were gradually added to the conical flask and stirred for 48 h, during which NHS and EDC acted as activators for functional groups. This mixture was heated at 65 ℃ for 12 h to remove THF, NHS, and EDC. Finally, the CDs-APhen complex was synthesized by vacuum drying at 60 ℃ for 24 h. The diagram of synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1.

To synthesize the CDs-APhen@SC filler, 1 g SC and 10 mL APTES were dispersed in a mixture of 400 mL ethanol and 100 mL deionized (DI) water. This mixture was stirred for 4 h, during which time the surface of SC particles was modified with the amino groups in APTES. The modified SC was separated by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min, washed twice with ethanol, and dried at 50 ℃ for 12 h. Then, the modified SC and CDs-APhen at a weight ratio of 1:3 were dispersed in 150 mL DI water and stirred for 6 h, centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min, and dried at 50 ℃ to obtain CDs-APhen@SC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PU coatings containing CDs-APhen@SC
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-APhen@SC was incorporated in the PU matrix to fabricate the coatings. PU and isocyanate (a curing agent) were mixed in a weight ratio of 85:100 and ultrasonicated for 20 min. Then, CDs-APhen@SC was dispersed in this mixture at 0, 1, 2, and 3 wt% relative to the PU matrix. After 1 h of stirring, the mixture was coated onto the steel plates using a scraper, and cured first in air for 12 h and then at 60 ℃ in an oven overnight. The coating thickness was 80–100 μm. According to the incorporated amount of CDs-APhen@SC, the as-prepared coatings were denoted as PU, CDs-APhen-1@SC/PU, CDs-APhen-2@SC/PU, and CDs-APhen-3@SC/PU, respectively.

TEM (JEM-2100 F, Jeol) and AFM (Dimension 3100, Veeco) were applied to characterize the morphology of CDs and CDs-APhen complex, which were deposited on the surface of carbon film supported on copper grids. The chemical structure was analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, VERTEX 70, Bruker) and UV-Vis (Lambda 750, PerkinElmer) spectra. The morphology and structure of CDs-APhen@SC were investigated using FTIR, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Merlin, Zeiss), and zeta potential measurements (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence between CDs/CDs-APhen and Fe2+/Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: 100 ppm of CDs and CDs-APhen solutions were prepared separately and divided into glass bottles. Solutions of FeCl2 and FeCl3·6 H2O were also prepared in DI water at concentrations of 0, 1×10−7, 1×10−6, 1×10−5, 1×10−4, 5×10−4, 1×10−3, 5×10−3 and 1×10−2 M. Subsequently, 30 μL of Fe2+/Fe3+ solutions were introduced into a glass bottle with 15 mL CDs or CDs-APhen solution. Fluorescence photographs were taken under UV light irradiation to evaluate the fluorescence quenching behavior. Photoluminescence spectroscopy was recorded by an FLS1000 Photoluminescence spectrometer. Under natural light, a Nikon digital camera D7200 was used to capture the color changes of CDs and CDs-APhen after their reaction with Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, respectively.

The electronic structure and adsorption pattern of CDs-APhen were studied theoretically using density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations [32,33]. For DFT, the Gaussian 09 program was used to calculate the energy and relative quantum chemical parameters of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The energy gap (ΔE = E LUMO - E HOMO), electronegativity (η), absolute electronegativity (χ), and the number of electrons transferred from the molecule to the metal (ΔN) were calculated.

The adsorption pattern of CDs+APhen and CDs-APhen complex was studied using MD simulation in the Forcite module within Material Studio 8.0. The 29.78×29.78×89.18 Å3 simulation box contained 6 layers of Fe (110), 1000 H2O, 18 H3O+, 18 Cl-, 5 CDs + 5 APhen (or 5 CDs-APhen complexes), and a 40-Å vacuum layer. Periodic boundary conditions and the COMPASS force field were used. The NVT ensemble integration method was employed to achieve high simulation quality, and the total simulation time was 2000 ps. The adsorption energy (E ads) and binding energy (E binding) of the corrosion inhibitor is calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2).

E total is the total system energy. E surf+sol and E inh+sol are the potential energy of Fe surface and solution, and the that of corrosion inhibitor and solution, respectively. E sol is the potential energy of solution.

To investigate the diffusion behavior of corrosive species in blank aqueous phase or inhibitor containing solutions, diffusion models were built using the MD simulation methods with COMPASS II force field. The simulation temperature was controlled at 298 K by the Andersen thermostat, and the pressure was atmospheric pressure (1.0×105 Pa). All simulations were performed under isothermal-isobaric (NPT) ensemble for 500 ps and canonical (NVT) ensemble for 1000 ps. Post-simulation, the ANALYSE component of the Forcite module was utilized. The diffusion coefficients (D) of the corrosive particles were determined by the mean square displacement [33].

Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to measure the inhibition behavior of corrosion inhibitors in 1 M HCl solution, using a DH7000 electrochemical workstation [34]. A conventional three-electrode system was adopted, containing the experimental material (1.0 cm2 exposed area), a platinum sheet, and a saturated calomel electrode. EIS data were collected at the open-circuit potential (OCP) from 100 kHz to 10 mHz frequency under a 10 mV sinusoidal voltage disturbance. PDP test was performed in a potential range of ± 250 mV vs. E OCP with a scan rate of 0.5 mV/s. The barrier and self-healing performances of the CDs-APhen@SC/PU coatings were assessed in HCl solution using EIS under the same experimental conditions. To mimic coating damage, a medical scalpel was used to make a scratch (approximately 80 μm × 2 cm) through the thickness of the coating.

After immersing Q235 steel samples in 1 M HCl solution with or without corrosion inhibitors, the surface morphology and roughness were measured using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, VK-X, Keyence). Furthermore, scratched PU coatings with and without CDs-APhen@SC were immersed in 0.1 M HCl solution for 60 days. Then, SEM and EDS were employed to analyze the surface morphology and elemental composition.

The morphologies and structures of CDs and CDs-APhen were analyzed using TEM and AFM, and the results are shown in Figs. 2 a-2d. The CDs exhibited a uniform spherical structure (size: 2–13 nm, average size: 6.63 nm). After grafting APhen onto the CDs, the CDs-APhen complex was evenly distributed, and the average particle size significantly increased to 16.99 nm. From the height profiles from the AFM images, the typical topographic height differences of CDs and CDs-APhen reached 4 and 6 nm, respectively. FTIR spectroscopy was used to investigate the chemical structures of CDs and CDs-APhen. As shown in Fig. 2 e, the FTIR spectrum of CDs exhibits characteristic peaks at 1185, 1357, 1600, 1709, and 3420 cm−1, corresponding to the C-OH stretching, C-N stretching, N-H in-plane bending, CO stretching, and O-H stretching vibrations, respectively [35,36]. APhen clearly shows the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the -NH2 group at 3205 and 3328 cm−1, respectively. For CDs-APhen, three new peaks appeared at 1280, 1532, and 1689 cm−1, which respectively represent the stretching vibrations of C-N, N-H, and CO in the amide group formed between APhen and CDs [37]. On the other hand, the stretching vibration peak of the -NH2 group in APhen disappeared in the complex. These results indicated that APhen successfully formed an amide group with CDs to produce the CDs-APhen complex. In the UV-Vis spectra in Fig. 2 f, CDs exhibited two absorption peaks at 240 and 340 nm, which were attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of the conjugate system and n-π* transition of the CN bond, respectively [38]. APhen illustrated absorption peaks at 230, 251, 282, and 331 nm [39]. The characteristic peaks of CDs-APhen showed a blue shift relative to those of APhen. The strong absorption peak at 282 nm disappeared, which can be explained by the destruction of p-π conjugation formed by the amino group and benzene rings on APhen. More importantly, the trapping of excitation energy by heteroatoms can lead to energy absorption, which is responsible for the strong fluorescence of CDs and CDs-APhen [40].

The corrosion inhibition effects of CDs and CDs-APhen were evaluated using the PDP method. Fig. 3 a shows the Tafel curves of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution alone, with 200 mg/L APhen, 200 mg/L CDs, 100 mg/L CDs + 100 mg/L APhen, or 200 mg/L CDs-APhen complex. There was an obvious negative shift in the polarization curves in the presence of corrosion inhibitors. The corrosion inhibitors also led to dramatic decreases in both the anodic and cathodic current densities, indicating that the corrosion inhibitors adsorbed on Q235 steel to simultaneously suppress the cathodic reduction of H+ and the anodic dissolution of steel [41]. The corrosion current density (i corr) of Q235 steel in HCl solution was 261.27 μA·cm−2, and this value decreased substantially to 102.85, 30.60, and 20.26 μA·cm−2 for APhen, CDs, and CDs+APhen containing solutions, respectively. Importantly, the corrosion mechanism changed in the presence of CDs-APhen complex, illustrating anodic passivation behavior. With the positive increase of potential from E corr (-0.576 V), the polarization curve of CDs-APhen passed through the activity region, transition region, and passivity region. After reaching the passivation potential (E pp) at −0.552 V, the corrosion current density value decreased rapidly from 61.65 μA/cm2 to a passivation current of 6.79 μA/cm2, indicating that the corrosion rate was dramatically declined on the metal substrate. The complete passivation film was formed at the passivation potential (E p) of −0.541 V. Within the passivity region, the current density increased slightly along with the increase of potential. The significantly reduced i corr values and passivation behaviors demonstrate favorable inhibitory effect of the CDs-APhen complex on the steel surface. CDs-APhen, with strong de-localization of electrons, promotes the formation of (M–In–OH)ads, (M–In)ads or (M–In–Cl-)ads complexes. The passive complexes film offers strong adsorption on the reactive sites of steel surface, which influence the anodic dissolution of metals [42–44].

To further validate the corrosion inhibition effect of CDs-APhen, EIS measurements were conducted in different solutions. Figs. 3 b and 3 d show the Nyquist plots of Q235 steel after immersion in different solutions for 6 and 24 h. In the Nyquist plots, the blank HCl solution shows the smallest diameter in the capacitive reactance arc during the entire immersion period, whereas any corrosion inhibitor increased this diameter. In the presence of APhen or CDs, the capacitive reactance arc was slightly larger than that of the blank sample. The addition of CDs+APhen mixture further increased the capacitive reactance arc diameter, and CDs-APhen led to the largest capacitive reactance arcs. Moreover, the diameter of the capacitive reactance arc increased when prolonging the immersion time from 6 to 24 h. Figs. 3 c and 3 e present the corresponding Bode diagrams. After 24 h of immersion, the magnitude of low-frequency impedance modulus at 0.01 Hz (|Z|0.01 Hz) was in the order of CDs-APhen＞CDs+APhen＞CDs＞APhen＞blank 1 M HCl. The |Z|0.01 Hz value of CDs-APhen coating sample (2071.7 Ω∙cm2) was approximately 67 times that of the blank HCl solution (31.0 Ω∙cm2), which can be explained by the gradual adsorption of CDs-APhen with the optimal inhibition effect to block the corrosion reaction. In the phase angle plots, the addition of inhibitors increased the phase angle values and widened the frequency range with maximum phase angles, indicating strong adsorption of inhibitors at the steel-solution interface [45].

To obtain an in-depth understanding of the EIS data, the electrical equivalent circuit (EEC) shown in the inset of Fig. 3 b was used to fit the data, where R s, R f, and R ct represent the solution resistance, film resistance, and charge transfer resistance, respectively. The constant phase elements CPE f and CPE dl represent the surface film capacitance and double-layer capacitance, respectively. R ct values in Fig. 3 f reflects the corrosion rate of the metal surface, with a larger R ct indicating slower corrosion [46]. The value of R ct in blank 1 M HCl solution was low at 23.5 Ω·cm2 at 6 h and 25.4 Ω·cm2 at 24 h, whereas it increased remarkably after adding the corrosion inhibitors. For APhen, the values are 250.0 Ω·cm2 at 6 h and 167.3 Ω·cm2 at 24 h, indicating noticeable corrosion inhibition. In comparison, the CDs showed stronger corrosion effects (390.3 Ω·cm2 at 6 h and 487.8 Ω·cm2 at 24 h). The mixture of CDs+APhen exhibited a synergistic effect that strengthened the corrosion inhibition effect, with R ct values of 820.3 Ω·cm2 at 6 h and 1560.8 Ω·cm2 at 24 h. Finally, the R ct value was the highest after adding the CDs-APhen complex (931.7 Ω·cm2 at 6 h and 1885.3 Ω·cm2 at 24 h). During the adsorption of corrosion inhibitors, the R f was the highest for the CDs-APhen sample and illustrated an increased trend (136.9 Ω·cm2 at 6 h and 212.3 Ω·cm2 at 24 h), which verified the positive effect of inhibitor layer formation on the corrosion inhibition of steel in HCl solution. CPE f was the smallest for the CDs-APhen sample, which was because CPE f is inversely proportional to the thickness of inhibitor film. Therefore, a reduction in CPE f value indicates the continuous growth of inhibitor layer. From the above results, adding CDs-APhen complex to 1 M HCl solution increased the R ct value by approximately two orders of magnitude and increased the R f value by approximately one order, revealing that complexation effectively strengthened the corrosion inhibition effect. Table 1

Fig. 4 shows CLSM images of Q235 steel surfaces after 24 h of immersion. Severe corrosion was observed on the steel exposed to 1 M HCl solution, with a surface roughness value of 3.303 μm. The corrosion inhibitors dramatically suppressed corrosion, and the surface roughness values decreased to 2.380, 1.447, 1.332, and 1.254 μm in the presence of APhen, CDs, CDs+APhen, and CDs-APhen, respectively. In the case of CDs-APhen, even polishing lines on the steel surface were retained after immersion. These surface observations confirm the optimal inhibition effect of CDs-APhen by forming a surface barrier film [47].

The XPS analysis of Q235 steel after a 24-hour immersion in 1 M HCl solution, both with and without 200 mg/L of CDs-APhen, was conducted to elucidate the chemistry of the adsorbed inhibitors on the steel surface. Fig. 5 a illustrates the comprehensive XPS spectra of the steel surface in both solutions. The spectrum reveals that the surface of Q235 steel submerged in 1 M HCl solution comprises elements such as Fe, O, and C, while a noticeable rise in the N elemental signal was observed for the sample containing CDs-APhen. Fig. 5 b-d detail the high-resolution peaks of N 1 s, Fe 2p3/2, and O 1 s. The N 1 s spectrum is deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at 398.7, 399.5, and 400.2 eV, corresponding to the N-C, N···Fe, and N+H bonds, respectively [36]. The Fe 2p3/2 high-resolution spectrum is resolved into four peaks representing Fe, Fe···Nx, Fe2O3, and FeOOH, with binding energies of 707.0, 710.5, 711.5, and 712.8 eV, respectively [41]. The presence of the N-Fe bond validates the interaction between CDs-APhen and steel, forming a protective film that impedes the corrosion process. Regarding the O 1 s spectrum, three peaks at 530.2, 531.8, and 533.2 eV were identified, corresponding to Fe2O3, C-O, and CO bonds, respectively [48]. These XPS findings substantiate the chemical interaction of CDs-APhen with the steel surface. This is in alignment with the outcomes from electrochemical assays and surface characterization studies.

In addition to the laboratory experiments, the corrosion inhibition behavior was theoretically studied using DFT and MD simulations [49]. The optimized structures and distributions of the frontier molecular orbitals of CDs, APhen, and CDs-APhen are shown in Fig. 6. For CDs and APhen, the HOMO was distributed throughout the molecular structure, and the LUMO was delocalized on the skeleton containing N. For the CDs-APhen complex, the HOMO was delocalized on the CDs part, and the LUMO existed on the CDs skeleton with N as well as a small part of APhen [50].

From literature, a higher E HOMO value (higher possibility for molecules to donate electrons) and a lower E LUMO value (stronger ability for molecules to accept electrons) are favorable for corrosion inhibition. The energy gap parameter between E LUMO and E HOMO (ΔE) can reflect the interaction between the molecule and metal surface, with a smaller ΔE indicating more stable adsorption of the inhibitor film. The quantum chemical parameters of the corrosion inhibitors are listed in Table 2. CDs-APhen had the highest E HOMO and lowest E LUMO values. The ΔE values were in the order of CDs > APhen > CDs-APhen, indicating that the CDs-APhen complex more readily adsorbs on metal surface and forms a stable barrier film to inhibit corrosion. The ΔN value reflects the number of electron transferred from the inhibitor to the metal. All ΔN values were positive, and the CDs-APhen complex had the largest ΔN, verifying that this complex is most likely to donate electron and reinforce the inhibitory effect against steel corrosion. Therefore, the DFT results support the experimentally observed trends in corrosion inhibition efficiency.

Next, inhibitor adsorption on the Fe surface was examined using MD simulations. From the adsorption equilibrium configurations in Fig. 7, all corrosion inhibitors are adsorbed in an equilibrium manner to ensure maximum coverage on the metal surface. E binding value was used to evaluate the corrosion inhibition efficiency. As shown in Table 3, the E binding of CDs-APhen complex on Fe (110) surface was 614.14 kcal·mol–1, whereas that of CDs+APhen was only 533.92 kcal·mol–1. In summary, the CDs-APhen complex shows stable adsorption on the steel substrate, generating a robust protective film against steel corrosion. We further investigated the diffusion behavior of corrosive species (H3O+, Cl−) in blank aqueous phase and with dispersed corrosion inhibitors. Fig. 8 shows the diffusion models of corrosive species in blank solution (H2O phase), CDs+APhen containing solution, and CDs-APhen containing solution. The calculated diffusion coefficients (D) of H3O+ and Cl− are shown in Table 4. It is evident that the D values of corrosive species in the blank solution were significantly higher than that in the inhibitor containing solutions, indicating that corrosive species easily diffuse in the solution and will reach the metal surface, leading to severe steel corrosion. Meanwhile, the diffusion coefficients of H3O+ and Cl− in the CDs-APhen containing solution were the smallest among the three models, suggesting that the synthesized complex inhibitor film can more effectively inhibit the diffusion of H3O+ and Cl− towards the steel surface. The adsorption pattern of CDs-APhen is depicted in Fig. 9. The physisorption occurs when inhibitors are protonated in HCl solution and interact with the chloride ions on the steel surface. The chemisorption is attributed to the lone pair of unprotonated heteroatoms, which can fill the unoccupied 3d orbitals of iron surface, and vice-versa. The retrodonation bond between the delocalized π-electrons of inhibitors and the steel surface also facilitate the adsorption of inhibitors.

After verifying the favorable corrosion inhibition property of the CDs-APhen complex, its ability to sense corrosion by detecting Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions was evaluated, using CDs as the reference. Figs. 10 a and 10 b show digital photographs of solutions containing 100 ppm CDs or CDs-APhen and different concentrations of Fe2+ or Fe3+ ions (0, 1×10−7, 1×10−6, 1×10−5, 1×10−4, 5×10−4, 1×10−3, 5×10−3, and 1×10−2 M). The CDs were dark yellow in color, and mixing them with Fe2+/Fe3+ failed to produce an obvious color change. In comparison, the CDs-APhen complex solution visibly changed to light red when the Fe2+ concentration reached 1×10−5 M, and the red color gradually deepened at higher Fe2+ concentrations. The CDs-APhen solution did not show any obvious color changes when encountering Fe3+ at low concentrations, became light red at 1×10−4 M Fe3+, and a higher Fe3+ concentration turned the solution to a yellow color due to the Fe3+ ions. These results indicate that the CDs-APhen complex can sensitively detect Fe2+ under natural light conditions.

Figs. 10 c and 10 d show photographs of the above solution samples under 365 nm UV light irradiation. The CDs emitted discernible fluorescence, the addition of Fe2+ had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity, while the fluorescence was quenched when the Fe3+ concentration exceeded 5×10–4 M. However, the fluorescence quenching effect was not apparent for sensitive detection of Fe3+. Compared to the CDs, the CDs-APhen complex showed brighter blue fluorescence emission and the quenching happened upon interaction with either Fe2+ or Fe3+. The fluorescent quenching was significant when the Fe2+ and Fe3+ concentration reached 1×10–4 M or higher. Moreover, Figs. 10 e and 10 f show that the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the CDs-APhen complex decreased dramatically with increasing metal ion concentration. Compared to the CDs, the CDs-APhen complex has a larger number of active surface groups for the adsorption of Fe2+ and Fe3+ to form non-fluorescent chelates, which causes the fluorescence quenching [33].

The CDs-APhen complex was directly applied to Q235 steel surface to verify its sensitivity to early corrosion. Fig. 11 a shows the optical images after immersing the steel in 1 M HCl solution for 10, 30, and 60 min, followed by dropping 1 g/L CDs-APhen solution onto the steel surface. An obvious red color appeared, and the color became more intense with the elapse of acid immersion time. This indicates that CDs-APhen can detect early corrosion of carbon steels by displaying color change. To confirm corrosion sensing based on the fluorescence quenching effect, a groove with a specific shape was created on the Q235 steel surface, and 0.1 M HCl solution was added to the groove. After waiting for 0, 5, 10, and 20 min, a drop of diluted 30% hydrogen peroxide solution was added to the groove to promote the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+ [47]. The CDs-APhen complex was then added to the groove, followed by irradiating the steel surface with 365 nm UV light. Fig. 11 b shows that the CDs-APhen complex can quickly and sensitively respond to Fe3+ ions and exhibit a fluorescence quenching effect. A longer contact with the HCl solution generates more Fe3+ ions, and the fluorescence quenching phenomenon thus became more evident.

Subsequently, CDs-APhen was anchored onto the surface of APTES-modified sericite (SC) and loaded into the coatings. Fig. 12 a shows that the Zeta potentials of CDs-APhen and SC are −17.55 and −9.84 mV, respectively. To enable electrostatic adsorption of CDs-APhen on SC, SC was modified with APTES to increase the Zeta potential to 12.62 mV. The positively charged modified SC and negatively charged CDs-APhen complex were combined via electrostatic attraction to create the CDs-APhen@SC coating filler [51]. The chemical structures of modified SC and CDs-APhen@SC were confirmed using FTIR spectroscopy. According to the spectra in Fig. 12 b, SC exhibited characteristic adsorption at 3617 and 1025 cm−1, which represented the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups from Si-OH and the Si-O group, respectively [52]. The peaks at 2928–2854 cm−1 corresponded to the C-H stretching vibration, verifying that APTES was successfully grafted onto the surface of SC [53]. In the spectrum of CDs-APhen@SC, new adsorption peaks appeared at 1643 and 1689 cm−1, which were assigned to the characteristic peaks of CDs-APhen. The morphology of CDs-APhen@SC was analyzed using SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 12 c, SC had a two-dimensional flake structure with a smooth and flat surface, and CDs-APhen@SC was covered by many CDs-APhen nanoparticles. The TEM images in Fig. 12 d present the Si, K, Al elements on the SC surface, and N and O signals demonstrate the adsorption of CDs-APhen. Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 investigates the release behavior of CDs from CDs-APhen@SC, which showed accelerated release in acid solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Barrier and self-healing properties of coatings
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the CDs-APhen@SC/PU coatings, CDs-APhen@SC was added to the PU resin at 0, 1, 2, and 3 wt% relative to the polymer matrix. The coatings’ barrier properties were investigated using EIS analysis. Fig. 13 shows the Bode plots of the PU, CDs-APhen-1@SC/PU, CDs-APhen-2@SC/PU, and CDs-APhen-3@SC/PU coatings after immersion in 1 M HCl solutions for different times. All coatings displayed capacitance characteristics within 7 d, and the |Z|0.01 Hz values were as high as 1011 Ω∙cm2. The PU, CDs-APhen-1@SC/PU, and CDs-APhen-2@SC/PU coatings maintained the capacitance characteristics after 60 d, and their |Z|0.01 Hz values were as high as 6.25×1010, 1.06×1011, and 2.52×1011 Ω∙cm2, respectively. However, when the CDs-APhen@SC content reached 3 wt%, the long-term protection performance decreased, as shown in Fig. 13 d: |Z|0.01 Hz was 1.35×1011 Ω∙cm2 at the beginning of immersion, and after 14 d resistance features appeared and |Z|0.01 Hz dropped to 6.35×109 Ω∙cm2. The impedance modulus continued to decrease with further immersion, indicating that the corrosive solution permeated the metal matrix continuously. After 60 d, |Z|0.01 Hz was only 2.74×108 Ω∙cm2, 3 orders of magnitude smaller than the initial value. These results demonstrate the positive effect of adding CDs-APhen@SC on the barrier property of coatings, and the optimal amount of addition was determined to be 2 wt% based on the experimental results. This can be ascribed to the favorable compatibility of CDs-APhen@SC in the coating, which prevents micropore formation during the coating preparation. Accordingly, the CDs-APhen@SC/PU coating exhibited prominent durability and could protect the steel substrate against corrosion during long-term immersion in an HCl solution. CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU was selected as the optimal coating for the subsequent evaluation.

EIS and surface characterization were conducted to examine the effect of adding CDs-APhen@SC filler on the self-healing of coating with scratches. Fig. 14 shows the Nyquist diagrams and Bode plots of scratched PU and CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coatings in 1 M HCl solution. Each coating showed two time constants that were ascribed to reactions at the damaged coating area and solution-metal interface. For the scratched PU coating, the diameter of capacitive reactance arc decreased during immersion, and the |Z|0.01 Hz value declined from 1.8×104 Ω·cm2 on day 1–2.6×103 Ω·cm2 on day 7. This is attributed to the gradual corrosion process on the metal surface over time and the delamination behavior from the initial defect coating area [54–56]. For the scratched CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coating, the |Z|0.01 Hz value declined much more slowly, and on day 7 it reached 2.4×104 Ω·cm2, still one order of magnitude higher than that of the neat PU coating. The EIS data were fitted using the EEC depicted in the inset of Fig. 14 a, which includes the solution resistance (R s), coating pore resistance (R c), coating capacitance (Q c), charge-transfer resistance (R ct), and double-layer capacitance (Q dl). R ct is an important parameter representing the corrosion activity of the exposed steel surface. Fig. 14 e shows the time-dependent R ct of the PU and CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coatings. The PU coating showed a relatively low R ct value of 3.2×103 Ω·cm2 after 1 d, and it decreased to 2.0×103 Ω·cm2 after 7 d, portending corrosion of the steel substrate. In contrast, the R ct value of CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coating remained above 5.0×103 Ω·cm2 within 7 days. Clearly, the incorporation of CDs-APhen@SC into the PU matrix improved corrosion resistance at the coating-steel interface. When the scratched CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coating was immersed in a 0.1 M HCl solution, CDs-APhen was slowly released and adsorbed on the metal surface to inhibit corrosion at the scratched area. Moreover, the labyrinth effect of the SC layer inhibited the penetration of corrosive medium and improved the barrier performance of the coating [57].

After the EIS tests, the scratched coatings were observed by CLSM and SEM to confirm the self-healing effect ( Fig. 15). Around the scratched area of the PU coating there were massive corrosion products and delamination. Around the scratched area of the CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coating, only a small amount of corrosion products were observed, and the delamination phenomenon was alleviated [58,59]. The EDS mapping results show that the exposed steel surface with PU coating exhibited intense signals of O (16.16 wt%) and Cl (3.90 wt%), implying that the substrate suffered severe corrosion. In comparison, the exposed surface with CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coating showed weaker signals of both O (12.47 wt%) and Cl (1.18 wt%) plus a stronger signal of Fe (79.71 wt%), indicating suppressed corrosion activity due to the adsorption of CDs-APhen@SC with corrosion inhibition property on the metal surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-reporting performance of the CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coating
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coating to report corrosion through color/fluorescence change was investigated by immersing this coating in HCl solution to accelerate the corrosion process. As shown in Fig. 16, a scratch of ∼100 μm in width was made on the coating surface. After 7 d of immersion, a light red color was observed near the scratch due to the interaction between CDs-APhen and Fe2+ ions generated during corrosion. Under UV irradiation, the fluorescence enhancement occurs in the freshly scratched areas of the coating owing to the reflectance change at the damaged area. After 7 d, a distinct fluorescence quenching effect with a black line was observed along the scratch, which was resulted from the interaction between CDs-APhen complex and the generated Fe2+/Fe3+ ions. Underfilm corrosion was clearly determined from the expansion of the region with quenched fluorescence. Therefore, the onset of early-stage corrosion can be accurately identified by monitoring the color and fluorescence emission on the coating surface.

A novel coating with self-healing and self-reporting properties was successfully developed by incorporating a CDs-APhen@SC filler in a PU matrix. The CDs-APhen complex was synthesized by chemically bonding APhen to CDs. TEM and AFM results confirmed the spherical structure and uniform distribution of CDs-APhen. Compared to CDs, APhen, and their mixture, the CDs-APhen complex with multiple surface functional groups exhibited anodic passivation and higher impedance moduli against steel corrosion. DFT and MD simulation results demonstrated that the good inhibitory behavior of CDs-APhen was due to its parallel adsorption on the metal interface with strong binding through the active sites of CDs-APhen. Moreover, the coloration when CDs-APhen is exposed to Fe2+ and the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs-APhen by Fe3+ allow the coating to sensitively reveal steel corrosion under both natural and UV light irradiation. Subsequently, the CDs-APhen complex was anchored to modified SC surface and incorporated in PU coating. The addition of 2 wt% CDs-APhen@SC filler into PU substantially improved the barrier property and self-healing performance of the coating, which was ascribed to good compatibility between the filler and coating matrix, corrosion inhibition property of CDs-APhen, and the layered structure of sericite. The |Z|0.01 Hz value of the intact CDs-APhen@SC-2/PU coating was on the order of 1011 Ω∙cm2 after 60 days of immersion in 1 M HCl solution. For the scratched coating, its higher R ct values, lower water absorption fraction, and significantly less corrosion products all demonstrate the excellent self-healing properties of this composite coating. Furthermore, the released CDs-APhen complex provided rapid and sensitive color and fluorescence responses to Fe2+/Fe3+ ions generated during steel corrosion, offering sensitive self-reporting during the initial corrosion period. The self-healing and self-reporting properties endow the coating with quick response to external damage and long-term corrosion protection.
